title: How tDarts Works - Updated Guide
subtitle: A practical and up-to-date walkthrough for daily use: login, club management, tournament operations, scorer access, and league points.

steps.title: Start here: login and account setup

sections.content: To use tDarts, first sign in. The platform now follows a Google-first login flow, while email + password is still available as an alternative.

sections.title: Recommended login path
content: Open the “Login” or “Register” page.
Use the Google button first for the fastest onboarding.
Switch to email + password only if that better fits your workflow.

note.content: For organizers, each person should use their own personal account instead of a shared club login.

sections.title: When to use email + password
content: If you do not want to use Google login.
If you already have an email/password account flow.
If your organization requires explicit account credentials.

sections.title: Profile and recovery
content: Manage profile data from your account page after login.
Follow verification steps when changing sensitive fields.
Use password reset from the login page if needed.

steps.title: Club management: roles and responsibilities
steps.description: Admin, moderator, member and safe operations

sections.title: Create and operate a club
content: Create your club from the “My Club” area if you are not assigned elsewhere.
Add members so operations are not blocked by a single person.
For healthy daily operation, keep multiple active organizers.
Important: members can only manage club operations after being promoted to moderator; otherwise they remain regular club members.
This role distinction is visible in player/member management views, making it easier to identify moderators versus normal members.

sections.title: Why club members matter
content: Tasks can be split across check-in, board support, communication, and supervision.
Personal accounts create clear accountability and cleaner logs.
Shared credentials create risk, confusion, and avoidable mistakes.

note.content: Do not run a club with one shared account for multiple people. Give each organizer their own account and role.

sections.title: Admin vs moderator
content: Admin: full control including role administration and critical club settings.
Moderator: day-to-day operations, tournament handling, and member workflows within scope.
Member: participation and basic use without organizer-level controls.

sections.title: Landing page, gallery, and blog
content: Customize your club landing page branding and public presentation.
If you provide a detailed club address, it appears on the Search Map tab as well.
Use gallery sections to publish event photos.
Use blog/news posts to keep players informed.

note.content: Active landing content improves visibility and helps players find accurate event information faster.

sections.title: Board management change
content: Boards are managed at tournament level.
Boards are managed per tournament, with tournament-specific settings.

steps.title: Tournament creation and editing
steps.description: Current creation flow and editable fields

sections.title: Where to create tournaments now
content: Create new tournaments from the club Tournaments view.
Create tournaments from the club Tournaments view.

sections.title: Creation steps
content: 1) Details: name, schedule, entry settings, location, format, participation mode.
A detailed tournament address is also shown on the Search Map tab.
2) Boards: add boards with the “Add board” action and clear names.
3) Settings: scoring, player limits, password, league linkage, and related options.
4) Save and continue tournament setup from the overview page.

note.content: Board count defines practical parallel match capacity and impacts group-stage planning.

sections.title: Current group constraints
content: Supported group size: 3-7 players per group.
At least 2 groups are required for group-stage setup.
Board count influences feasible group distribution and scheduling.

note.content: Before generating groups, verify checked-in player count, board count, and selected format.

sections.title: What you can edit later
content: From Tournament Overview, use the “Edit / Szerkesztés” flow.
You can adjust board-related and key tournament fields based on current status rules.

steps.title: Legs configuration (Admin tab)
steps.description: Set per-group and per-round legs-to-win after generating groups or the bracket

sections.content: After generating groups or the knockout bracket, admins and moderators can configure how many legs a player must win for each match. This is done from the Admin tab on the tournament page.

sections.title: Why configure legs after generation?
content: Group size varies — groups with 6 or 7 players often need more legs than groups with 3 or 4.
Knockout rounds are not created until the bracket is generated, so per-round config must happen post-generation.
This approach gives organizers real data (actual group sizes and round counts) to make the right decision.

sections.title: Per-group legs (Group Stage)
content: In the Admin tab, the Legs Configuration panel shows one row per board.
Each board can have a different legs-to-win value — for example Board 1 might have 3 legs and Board 2 might have 5 legs.
Use the 'Set all boards' bulk action to apply the same value to every group at once.
Leave a row empty to allow the scorer to choose freely on that board.

note.content: Changes auto-save on selection. Each row shows a saved indicator after the action completes.

sections.title: Per-round legs (Knockout)
content: After the bracket is generated, a Knockout Rounds section appears in the same panel.
Each round (Quarter-Final, Semi-Final, Final, etc.) can have its own legs value.
If the bracket is cancelled and regenerated, knockout legs config is automatically cleared to prevent stale round-number mismatches.
Re-configure knockout legs after generating a new bracket.

sections.title: Enforcement
content: When a legs value is set for a group or round, the scorer screen locks the legs selector for regular scorers.
Admins and moderators can override the value on the board — an override indicator shows the organizer-configured value as context.
The server enforces the configured value: a non-admin scorer who submits a different value receives an error.

note.content: Config applies to all unstarted matches in the group or round. Matches already in progress are not retroactively changed.

sections.title: Registration and check-in
content: Players can register from the tournament page when registration is open.
Organizers can add players manually (registered or guest).
Group/knockout generation relies on checked-in players, so check-in must be accurate.

sections.title: Group stage and knockout
content: Group generation is available in automatic and manual modes.
Knockout stage can be handled in automatic or manual mode depending on tournament setup.
If tie scenarios require intervention, organizers can apply controlled manual adjustments.

sections.title: Operational discipline
content: Run critical actions only after quick data checks.
Use notifications and updates to keep players aligned.
Always confirm status context before changing phase/state.

steps.title: Scoring and board access
steps.description: Current scorer entry points and confirmation behavior

sections.title: How players/staff open scorer
content: Use the tournament header entry: “Táblák / író program”.
Or open a specific board directly from the boards list.
This is the current recommended path to the active scoring surface.

note.content: Use the tournament header “Táblák / író program” entry point or the board-list open button to access scorer.

sections.title: Confirmation modals are real save checkpoints
content: Leg-end and match-end confirmations are operational commit points.
After confirmation, data is saved and should be treated as finalized for that step.
Do not skip confirmations blindly.

note.content: Always verify players, score context, and outcome before pressing confirm.

sections.title: If a mistake happens
content: Immediate rollback options are limited.
Corrections typically require tournament manager action with proper permissions, usually after match completion.

steps.title: Leagues and points
steps.description: Current point systems and finished tournament attachment

sections.title: League setup
content: Create leagues from the club Leagues area.
Choose a point system during setup and review it in league settings.

sections.title: Predefined point systems
content: Platform - placement-based points with configurable rank values and group-dropout points.
Remiz Christmas Series - participation + group performance + final placement model.
DartsBarlang OnTour - fixed placement table.
Goldfisch - fixed placement system with special handling for smaller tournaments.

note.content: Follow the selected point system configuration for league scoring.

sections.title: Attach finished tournaments
content: Only finished tournaments can be attached to a league.
When attaching, you can choose to calculate points or attach without point calculation.

sections.title: Manual point adjustments
content: Admins/moderators can apply manual point corrections with justification.
Keep point changes documented for transparency.

steps.title: Recommended daily workflow
steps.description: Simple process for smooth event operation

sections.title: Before tournament start
content: Validate schedule and tournament core settings.
Prepare board names and roles for organizers.
Test scorer entry via header and board list.

sections.title: During tournament
content: Generate phases only with correct check-in data.
Handle confirmation dialogs carefully on every leg/match checkpoint.
Escalate inconsistencies immediately to organizer staff.

sections.title: After tournament
content: Confirm final status and published results.
For leagues, attach finished tournaments with or without point calculation as needed.
Post updates through club news/blog channels.

sections.title: Frequent mistakes
content: One shared club account used by multiple organizers.
Blindly confirming scoring modals.
Generating groups with incomplete check-in.
Using unclear board naming conventions.

sections.title: Best practice
content: Assign role-based personal accounts to all organizers.
Run a short pre-action check for players, boards, and status.
Record who executed critical control actions.

note.content: A short 2-minute organizer sync before each major phase can prevent most operational errors.

steps.title: Sharing: clubs, tournaments, galleries
steps.description: Share clubs and tournaments with links and QR

sections.title: Share types
content: Registration share: routes users through sign-in and then redirects to the shared destination.
Public share: opens the shared destination directly without sign-in.

sections.title: Sharing a club
content: Use the share action from the club page.
The system provides printable QR and shareable links.
The link points to the club's public information and tournament context.
Registration link: after login, users land on the club page.
Public link: opens the club page directly.

sections.title: Sharing a tournament
content: Use the share action from the tournament page.
Printable QR and direct links are available.
Shared links open the tournament landing context.
Registration link: after login, users are redirected to the tournament.
Public link: opens tournament details directly.

sections.title: Using QR codes
content: QR codes can be printed and placed on-site.
Players can scan quickly with their phones.
They provide fast access to tournament-related pages.
Especially useful in larger venues.

sections.title: What is MMR?
content: MMR (Matchmaking Rating) is a performance-based ranking metric.
It changes according to tournament outcomes and match performance.
More tournament data usually leads to a more stable rating.

sections.title: How MMR is calculated
content: MMR change is influenced by multiple factors, with placement carrying major weight.
Placement/performance weighting can vary by tournament size.
list: Small tournament (8 or fewer players): Placement 60%, Performance 40%
Very large tournament (64+ players): Placement 75%, Performance 25%

subsections.title: Performance components
content: Match win ratio.
Leg win ratio.
Average scoring relative to tournament average.

subsections.title: Tournament size impact
content: Larger tournaments can result in larger MMR movement.
The model accounts for field size when evaluating outcomes.

sections.title: MMR tiers
content: Players are grouped into tiers by current MMR value.

sections.title: Viewing rankings
content: Use the search/ranking area to view players sorted by MMR.
Player cards display current MMR and rank tier.
Rankings refresh after tournament completion.

note.content: MMR works best as a trend indicator and should be read together with match and leg stats.

steps.title: Local match mode
steps.description: Quick practice and ad-hoc play without tournament setup

sections.title: When to use local match
content: Practice sessions and friendly games outside tournament operations.
Fast start without creating full tournament structures.
Training users on scorer interactions before event day.

sections.title: How to start local match
content: Open the board interface and choose the “Local match” start action.
You can do this from the main /board page, and also from the /board/[tournamentId] header area.
Set legs-to-win and starting score in the setup modal.
Press “Start match” to enter the local scorer screen immediately.

sections.title: During local match
content: Scoring works similarly to tournament match scoring.
In settings (gear icon), you can update key match parameters or restart the match when needed.
Use leg/match confirmations carefully here as well.

sections.title: Operational tip
content: Before tournaments, run a short local test match on your event devices.
It reduces errors during real scoring sessions.

steps.title: PWA installation
steps.description: Use tDarts like a mobile app

sections.title: What is PWA?
content: tDarts can be installed as a Progressive Web App on phones and tablets.
It gives app-like behavior without app-store installation.

sections.title: Install on Android
content: Open tDarts in Chrome.
Use “Install app” or “Add to home screen” from the browser menu.
Launch it later from the home screen icon.

sections.title: Install on iOS
content: Open tDarts in Safari.
Use Share -> “Add to Home Screen”.
Launch it from the created icon.

sections.title: PWA advantages
content: Fast launch and app-like usage during events.
Full-screen interface, useful on scorer tablets.
Stable daily routine for organizers with one-tap access.

sections.title: Practical use cases
content: Fixed scorer tablets at venue boards.
Quick organizer access from mobile.
Convenient board/local-match usage in app-like mode.

sections.title: Full-screen mode
content: PWA launch opens a cleaner full-screen interface.
This is especially useful on tournament tablets.

note.content: Before event day, verify installation and launch behavior on all designated scorer/organizer devices.

cta.title: Ready to run your next event?
cta.description: Open your club, create the tournament with board setup, and operate scoring through the current validated flow.
